coredata.dc:title: Carbonized human hair derived carbon dots for detection of clozapine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yongqing
Wan, Runlan
Wang, Junji
Liu, Qiuyi
Tian, Meng
Wang, Luchun
Yang, Yulian
Zou, Yuemeng
Luo, Yuanning
Ke, Famin
Zhou, Quan
Wang, Dandan
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Clozapine (CLZ) is known as the most effective antipsychotic medication for schizophrenia. However, low dosage or over dosage of CLZ is adverse to the treatment of Schizophrenia. Thus, it is necessary to develop effective detection method for CLZ. Recently, due to the advantages such as excellent optical properties, good photobleachability and sensitivity, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensors for the detection of target analytes have drawn a great deal of attention. In this work, blue fluorescent CDs (Named as B-CDs) with quantum yield (QY) as high as 38% were obtained by using carbonized human hair as source material through one-step dialysis method for the first time. B-CDs showed obvious graphite-like structure with an average of 1.76 nm, containing abundant functional groups such as -C=O, amino N and C-N on the surface of carbon cores. Optical analysis showed that the B-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission property with maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm. Moreover, B-CDs were further applied as a fluorescence sensor to the detection of CLZ. The B-CDs based sensor exhibited a good quenching response by CLZ through the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism with a limit of detection of 67 ng/mL, which was much lower than the minimal effective concentration in blood (0.35 μg/mL). Finally, to test the practical application value of the developed fluorescence method, the determination of the content of CLZ in tablets and the concentration in blood was carried out. Compared with the results of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, it can be found that the constructed fluorescence detection method showed high accuracy and had great application potential in the detection of CLZ. Additionally, the results of cytotoxicity experiment showed that B-CDs had low cytotoxicity, which laid the foundation for the subsequent application of B-CDs in biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized human hair
Carbon dots
Clozapine
Sensitive detection
Tablets
Blood samples

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study the anti-interference capability and sensitivity of B-CDs to clozapine
Determination of CLZ in actual samples
Determination of CLZ in human blood
Stability and anti-interference capability of B-CDs
Fluorescent sensitivity of B-CDs towards CLZ
Application of B-CDs in blood samples
Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan (Grant No. 23NSFSC5092), a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-B. TEM image of B-CDs. C. Size distribution of B-CDs. D. XRD spectra of B-CDs powders. E. Raman spectrum of B-CDs. F. FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs. G. XPS survey spectrum of B-CDs.

A. UV–vis absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) spectra of B-CDs. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (Insets showed the B-CDs solution under visible light and under UV light of 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A. pH stability of B-CDs. B. Salt stability of B-CDs. C. Temperature stability of B-CDs. D. Time stability of B-CDs. E. The influence of the metal ions on the fluorescence of B-CDs. F. The influence of the other drugs on the fluorescence of B-CDs.
A. Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs with CLZ at different concentrations. B. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of CLZ.
A. Fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs and B-CDs with CLZ. B. Stern-Volmer curves of CLZ at different temperatures. C. Zeta potential analysis of B-CDs and B-CDs with CLZ.
Determination of the amount of clozapine in tablets (n = 5).
Determination of the concentration of clozapine in blood (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Carbonized human hair derived carbon dots for detection of clozapine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Clinical Medicine, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Clinical Medicine, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue fluorescent CDs (B-CDs) with quantum yield (QY) as high as 38% were obtained by using carbonized human hair as source material through one-step dialysis method for the first time. B-CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity for clozapine (CLZ). Moreover, B-CDs based sensor can be effectively applied to the detection of CLZ in tablets and blood samples. The results of fluorescent methods were comparable to those of HPLC methods.

Clozapine (CLZ) is known as the most effective antipsychotic medication for schizophrenia. However, low dosage or over dosage of CLZ is adverse to the treatment of Schizophrenia. Thus, it is necessary to develop effective detection method for CLZ. Recently, due to the advantages such as excellent optical properties, good photobleachability and sensitivity, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensors for the detection of target analytes have drawn a great deal of attention. In this work, blue fluorescent CDs (Named as B-CDs) with quantum yield (QY) as high as 38% were obtained by using carbonized human hair as source material through one-step dialysis method for the first time. B-CDs showed obvious graphite-like structure with an average of 1.76 nm, containing abundant functional groups such as -C=O, amino N and C-N on the surface of carbon cores. Optical analysis showed that the B-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission property with maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm. Moreover, B-CDs were further applied as a fluorescence sensor to the detection of CLZ. The B-CDs based sensor exhibited a good quenching response by CLZ through the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism with a limit of detection of 67 ng/mL, which was much lower than the minimal effective concentration in blood (0.35 μg/mL). Finally, to test the practical application value of the developed fluorescence method, the determination of the content of CLZ in tablets and the concentration in blood was carried out. Compared with the results of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, it can be found that the constructed fluorescence detection method showed high accuracy and had great application potential in the detection of CLZ. Additionally, the results of cytotoxicity experiment showed that B-CDs had low cytotoxicity, which laid the foundation for the subsequent application of B-CDs in biological systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Schizophrenia, one of the serious chronic weakening diseases, has gradually affected the physical and mental health of >20 million people especially young people around the world. This disease is characterized by distortions of consciousness, perception, emotions, and behavior [1], and many people have suicidal tendencies. Thus, the treatment of this disease is of great significance. Till now, clozapine (CLZ) is known as the most effective antipsychotic medication for schizophrenia and it is also the only one that the efficacy can be predicted by a blood test (0.35–1 μg/mL) [2,3]. However, low dosage and over dosage of CLZ are adverse to the treatment of schizophrenia. Specifically, CLZ will be failed to combat schizophrenia effectively at low doses. While, overuse of CLZ therapy can lead to serious adverse effects such as agranulocytosis, leukopenia myocardial depression, arrhythmia, liver damage and even death [4]. Therefore, in order to ensure the effectiveness and safety of CLZ, it is necessary to strictly monitor the content of CLZ in drugs and the concentration in blood.

To address this issue, various methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5,6], gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [7], electrochemical method [8] have already been developed. Although these methods are relatively effective, they also have some obvious disadvantages, such as requiring expensive equipment and professional technical operations, as well as being time-consuming and expensive. Ascribed to the preponderances of simple operation steps, low-cost, low time consumption and cheap instrument, fluorescence detection method has been widely favored as the most promising detection method for various drugs [9–13]. Especially, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of carbon nanomaterials that show various distinctive advantages including excellent fluorescence properties, versatile surface engineering properties, high electron transfer capability, simple synthesis steps, good biocompatibility and low cost [14–16]. Due to these advantages, CDs acting as a new fluorescence sensor element which used for the detection of different analytes have been getting increased attention [17,18]. However, no study has been reported on the fluorescence detection of CLZ, and thus it is of great value and significance to develop fluorescence analysis method, especially fluorescence analysis method based on CDs, to detect CLZ.

As far as we all know, the establishment of fluorescence detection method based on CDs for CLZ requires the selection of appropriate precursors to prepare CDs. Traditionally, the preparation of CDs use chemical compounds (e.g. m-phenylenediamine [19], critic acid and thiourea [20], citric acid and urea [21]) as precursors coupled with organic solvents (e.g. toluene, chloroform, DMF) as reaction solvents. The usage of above-mentioned chemical compounds and toxic solvents are harmful to the environment and humans, so the synthesis of CDs from green precursors through green synthesis method has received considerable attention.In recent years, the synthesis of CDs by using traditional Chinese medicine (TCMs) especially from charcoal TCMs as green precursors has attracted more and more interests [22,23]. Charcoal TCMs is a kind of characteristic TCMs with pharmacological action formed after high temperature carbonization of different basic TCMs. Because the preparation method (high temperature carbonization) of charcoal TCMs is similar with the preparation method of CDs through high temperature pyrolysis, the researchers therefore believe that charcoal TCMs should exist CDs. Actually, researchers have already isolate CDs from charcoal TCMs by means of dialysis [22,24,25]. In comparison with the solvothermal synthesis method (chemical compounds as precursors) and the hydrothermal synthesis method of (TCMs as precursors), the dialysis method with charcoal TCMs as sources is not only going green and environmental protection, but also simply in the preparation method. Regrettably, most of these researches are focused on studying the pharmacological activity of the CDs from charcoal TCMs [26,27], few researches are focused on the application of the CDs derived from charcoal TCMs in the sensing field.

Inspired by above-mentioned things, in present study, a facile one-step dialysis method was developed and applied to the directly separation of blue fluorescent CDs (Named as B-CDs) with the quantum yield (QY) of 38% from carbonized human hair for the first time. The structure and optical properties of the as-prepared B-CDs were evaluated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), X-ray diffraction spectrometer (XRD), Raman, ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–vis) and fluorescence analysis. To evaluate the stability of B-CDs, pH, salt concentration, temperature and time stabilities of were systematically studied. Further, by a fluorescent detection method which used B-CDs as the fluorescent sensing element, was successfully developed for the detection of CLZ. The method showed good selectivity and sensitivity, and can be effectively used for the detection of CLZ content in actual drug (e.g. tablet) and for the monitoring of the concentration of CLZ in blood. To deeply understand the interaction between B-CDs and CLZ, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was also studied systematically. Besides, in order to better apply B-CDs in the future, cytotoxicity evaluation was also preliminarily carried out.

The hair was collected from a local barbershop (Luzhou, Sichuan Province). Clozapine was purchased from Beijing Wokai Biotechnology Co., LTD. Stock solutions (1 mg/mL in methanol) of diazepam, cocaine, midazolam, estazolam, alprazolam, heroin and O6-monoacetylmorphine (6-MAM) (Structures shown in Figure S1 in Supporting Information ) were obtained from Cerilliant Corporation (Round Rock, TX, USA). HepG2 cells were purchased from the Cell Center of the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), 3- (4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, purity > 98%), trypsin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Penicillin–streptomycin was purchased from Beijing Dingguo biotechnology co. ltd. Organic solvents and reagents related to ions were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents in the experiments were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were made with deionized water.

Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves were heated in an electric blast drying oven (Yamato DKN412C). The fluorescence spectra were gained on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). The UV absorption spectra were obtained by using an UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). TEM images were gained using a JEOL 2100F (JEOL) transmission electron microscopy. The FT-IR spectra were acquired by an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The elemental compositions of CDs were acquired by using a K-Alpha + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). XRD measurement was acquired on Empyrean multifunctional high resolution X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical-3B.V.). Raman analysis was conducted through a Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer. Contact angle experiment was conducted on FCA 2000A AFES Contact angle measurement instrument (Shanghai Aifei Precision Instrument Co., Ltd).

The hair was washed with pure water to remove impurities and then repeatedly soaked and cleaned with NaOH solution for three times to remove grease. After these steps, the hair was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h before use. Next, the clean hair was wrapped in tinfoil and placed in a crucible. Thereafter, the calcining temperature was raised to 300 ℃ by Muffle furnace and kept at 300 ℃ for 1 h. Let the carbonized human hair cool down to the room temperature and grind with a mortar. The obtained powder was then treated in a water bath at 90 ℃ for 3 h. After that, the obtained solution was centrifuged (7000 rpm for 20 min) and the remaining powder was filtered through 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membrane. After dialyzing with 1000 Da dialysis membrane for 72 h, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried to obtain brown and yellow powder (B-CDs).

The stability of B-CDs was systematically studied before fluorescence detection. (1) pH stability. B-CDs were dissolved in water with different pH values (1–14) at a concentration of 1 mg/L. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was determined by using a fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm. (2) Ionic strength stability. 300 μL of NaCl solution with different concentrations was separately added into 2.7 mL of B-CDs solution (1 mg/L), and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was recorded. (3) Temperature stability. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs solution (1 mg/L) was determined at different temperature including 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45 and 50 ℃. (4) Time stability. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs solution (1 mg/L) was determined at different time point within 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the anti-interference capability and sensitivity of B-CDs to clozapine
ce:section.ce:para: To study the anti-interference capability of B-CDs, different metal ions and compounds were selected as the interfering substances. (1) Interference experiment of different metal ions. 300 μL of different ion solution (including Fe2+, Er3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Eu3+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mn4+, Na+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Bi3+ and Al3+, 1 mM) was separately added into 2.7 mL of B-CDs solution (1 mg/L). The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs in the presence of different metal ions was studied by fluorescence spectrometer under the same conditions. (2) Interference experiment of different compounds. 300 μL of compound solution (including diazepam, cocaine, midazolam, estazolam, alprazolam, heroin and 6-MAM, 0.2 mg/mL) at different concentration was separately added into 2.7 mL of B-CDs (1 mg/L) solution. After incubating for 1 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. All measurements were repeated three times and then averaged. Besides, to sensitive detection of CLZ, 300 μL of clozapine solution at different concentration (2 μg/mL-1000 μg/mL) was separately added into 2.7 mL of B-CDs (1 mg/L) solution. The next steps were same with the anti-interference capability experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CLZ in actual samples

ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets (Total weight of 0.97 g) with the CLZ standard dosage of 25 mg per tablet were ground into powder with a mortar. Then, 97 mg of the powder was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol to prepare a solution (2.5 mg/mL of clozapine). After that, the solution was ultrasonic mixed and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and diluted to 25 μg/mL. The fluorescence intensity was measured and the recovery rate was calculated according to experimental operation in the section of 2.5. The experiment was repeated five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CLZ in human blood
ce:section.ce:para: The human blood samples for the experiments were provided by the judicial expertise center of Southwest Medical University, which was the remaining blank blood after the judicial expertise. In details, 10 μL of CLZ standard solution with different concentration was separately added into 100 μL of blank blood sample. After mixing with vortex, the protein was precipitated by using 400 μL of acetonitrile. Then, the mixed solution was centrifuged at a high speed of 10,000 r/min for 10 min. After filtration, the solvent was dried by nitrogen blowing, and the residue was then redissolved with 100 μL ethanol. Finally, after filtration with 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane, HPLC was used for analysis. Since CLZ was not present in the actual blood sample, the standard addition method was used. The samples with blood concentrations of 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 μg/mL were determined by HPLC and the recovery rates were calculated. The standard addition method was also used to evaluate the usefulness of fluorescence detection method. The sample pretreatment of fluorescence method was consistent with the above steps, and the standard curve was drawn. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the added sample was measured and the recovery rate was calculated. The experiment was performed five times in parallel and the results of two methods were compared.

HepG2 cells were added into 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃ in a cell incubator containing 5% CO2. Then, different concentration (0–1000 μg/mL) of B-CDs were separately added into wells and incubated for another 24 h. After that, 10 μL of MTT with the concentration of 5 mg/mL was separately added into each well and incubated for 4 h. Next, all solutions were removed from wells and 150 μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the formazan complex. Finally, the absorbance was determined at 570 nm through a microplate reader after shaking the plate for 10 min.
The preparation and application processes of B-CDs were shown in Scheme 1 .

The morphological characteristics of B-CDs were observed by TEM. Fig. 1
A-B revealed that B-CDs showed relatively uniform spherical structures and displayed lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.21 nm. This lattice space was in consistent with the graphite (100) plane, demonstrating that B-CDs had graphite-like structure [28–30]. The particle size of B-CDs ranged from 1.00 nm to 2.60 nm with an average particle size of about 1.76 nm (Fig. 1 C). XRD analysis was applied to identify the crystallization property of B-CDs. Fig. 1 D revealed that the XRD pattern of B-CDs showed a diffraction peak existed at about 2θ = 20°, which was matched with the diffraction peak of the (002) diffraction pattern of graphite carbon. This phenomenon revealed that B-CDs contained sp2 hybrid carbon core and had graphite-like structure [21,31]. Besides, Raman spectral analysis was conducted to explore the molecular picture of the carbonaceous nanostructure of B-CDs. Fig. 1 E showed that B-CDs exhibited two obvious broad peaks around 1372 and 1575 cm−1, which were corresponded to the D and G bands, revealing the presence of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [32].

To deeply understand the chemical compositions of B-CDs, FT-IR and XPS were carried out. Fig. 1 F displayed that the characteristic peaks in FT-IR spectrum at 3415 cm−1, 2915/2850 cm−1 were assigned to O–H/N–H and saturation C–H stretching vibrations [33,34]. The peaks at 1600 cm−1, 1447 cm−1, 1361 cm−1 and 1108 cm−1 were separately ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C=O/C=N, C=C, C-N= and C-O/C-N [35]. In the full scan spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1 G), three typical peaks located at 284.97 eV, 399.92 eV, 532.78 eV, originated from C1s, N 1s and O 1s. The contents of them were calculated as 91.72%, 5.06% and 3.22%, respectively. Specifically, the C 1s spectrum ( Fig. S2A ) had five significant peaks for C=C, C–N/C–C, C–H, C–O, C=O chemical bonds at 284.5 eV,284.9 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.1 eV and 287.8 eV, respectively. The N 1s spectra ( Fig. S2B ) revealed two peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.1 eV, which can be separately belonged to amino N and graphite N. The O 1s spectra ( Fig. S2C ) can be deconvoluted into two main peaks at 532.4 eV and 532.9 eV, demonstrating that B-CDs contained C–O and C=O. In general, these all results demonstrated that the surfaces of B-CDs were full of various functional groups such as N–H, O–H,–C=O and C-O, which not only improved the possibility of luminescence, but also increased the hydrophilicity of B-CDs. Besides, the hydrophilicity of B-CDs was further measured by using contact angle experiment. Figure S3 revealed that the value of water contact angle of B-CDs was determined as 33.96°, indicating that B-CDs showed hydrophilic property.

The optical properties of B-CDs were carried out by utilizing the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption. Fig. 2
A demonstrated that a clear absorption peak of B-CDs existed at approximately 285 nm, which was corresponded to the n − σ* transition of C–O and C–N bonds. There also existed a weak absorption band at approximately 330 nm, which could be attributed to the n − π* electron transition of C=O/C=N bonds [36]. The fluorescence properties of the B-CDs showed that B-CDs emitted stable blue strong fluorescence in solution under ultraviolet light (Insert of Fig. 2 B). B-CDs presented excitation dependent fluorescence emission, a typical characteristic of CDs, and maximal fluorescence emission of the B-CDs was 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 2 B). These good optical properties made B-CDs have great application value in the follow-up fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and anti-interference capability of B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to improve the detection performance of B-CDs on CLZ and eliminate the possible interference in the subsequent actual sample analysis, the stability and anti-interference capability of B-CDs were explored firstly. Fig. 3
A revealed that B-CDs maintained good stability in the pH range of 3–10, demonstrating its good acid and alkaline resistances. Salt and temperature also showed little influences on the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs, further demonstrating the good stability of B-CDs (Fig. 3 B-C). Followed, by analyzing the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs placed under different time (0–6 h) under lighting condition, it can be obviously seen that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs decreased slightly, indicating that B-CDs had good photobleaching resistance (Fig. 3 D). Further, to explore the anti-interference capability of B-CDs, different metal ions and drugs were applied as the interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 3 E-3F, metal ions showed little influences on the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs at high concentration of 100 μM, proving that B-CDs can maintain high anti-interference ability in the actual sample system containing such metal ions. Besides, it is also obviously seen that compared with the fluorescence quenching effect of CLZ, the fluorescence quenching effect of other drugs on B-CDs can be ignored at the concentration of 20 μg/mL, indicating that B-CDs showed outstanding anti-interference ability and good selectivity for CLZ. The advantage of this selectivity can be even more obvious when tested at lower concentrations in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensitivity of B-CDs towards CLZ
ce:section.ce:para:
Different concentration of 0.3 mL of CLZ was separately added into B-CDs solution (2.7 mL) under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 4
A, with the increase of the concentration of CLZ, the emission peak of B-CDs decreased gradually, and a good linear relationship value can be found in the concentration range of 200 ng/mL to 100 μg/mL. The linear regression equation was F0/F = 0.007[C] + 1.028 (R2 = 0.9948), where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs in the absence and presence of CLZ, respectively (Fig. 4 B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 67 ng/mL according to the formula of 3σ/ slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and slope is the slope of the correction curve). It can be known that the B-CDs based sensing method was not only developed for the first time for the sensitive detection of CLZ, but also the sensor platform showed satisfactory sensitivity. These all results demonstrated that B-CDs had the potential to detect CLZ sensitively.

In order to assess the application value of the as-proposed B-CDs based sensing platform in actual samples, tablets were used as the actual sample. Firstly, fluorescence detection method based on B-CDs for the detection of CLZ in tablets was constructed through the method described above in section 2.6.1. The results listed in Table 1 showed that the content of CLZ in per tablet was detected as 25.84 mg by fluorescence detection method, which was 103.26% of the labeled amount (25 mg). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the method was 0.73%. To verify the practicability of the fluorescence detection method, the content of CLZ in tablet was also detected by using HPLC method. As shown in Table 1, the recovery of CLZ in tablets was 99.23% with RSD of 0.42%. It can be obviously known that the detection result of HPLC method was consistent with the result of fluorescence detection method, further indicating the effectiveness of the as-developed fluorescence method. As far as we all know, according to the Chinese Pharmacopoeia, the content of CLZ in CLZ tablet should be 90.00% to 110.00% of the labeled amount. The results here were in line with the requirements of Chinese pharmacopoeia, further indicating that the established fluorescence method had good practical application value in the detection of CLZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of B-CDs in blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the detection of the concentration of CLZ in blood samples is important for safe drug use. Low concentration of CLZ in blood (<0.35 μg/mL) has poor therapeutic effect on schizophrenia. While, the high concentration of CLZ (>1 μg/mL) is easy to cause side effects. Therefore, the monitoring of the CLZ concentration in blood is of great clinical significance. Herein, B-CDs based fluorescence detection method was also employed to monitor the concentration of CLZ in blood samples, and HPLC method was also used to compare and evaluate the effectiveness of the fluorescence detection method. Considering the effective concentration of CLZ, 0.5, 1 and 2 μg/mL (Concentration in blood) were selected as the concentrations, and the above pretreatment and detection steps (In section 2.6.2) were adopted. The results which listed in Table 2 showed that the recoveries of CLZ in blood samples were calculated as 92.92%-102.45% with RSDs in range of 1.41%-7.91% by using B-CDs based fluorescence method. Moreover, the recoveries of CLZ which determined by using HPLC method were in range of 98.6%-102.30% with RSDs of 4.82%-6.49%. The consistency of the results of the two methods indicated that the constructed B-CDs based fluorescence detection method had good practical application potential in CLZ detection in complex biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To deeper understanding of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs - CLZ system, the fluorescence lifetimes, UV–vis analysis and Zeta potential were conducted. It is well-known that internal filtration effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET), static quenching and dynamic quenching are the main quenching mechanisms. If the quenching mechanism includes FRET, PET, and dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs with or without quenching agents will change [37,38]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs with and without the addition of CLZ were studied at first. Fig. 5
A displayed that there was almost no difference in the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs with and without CLZ, indicating that the quenching mechanism may not be FRET, PET and dynamic quenching, but possibly IFE or static quenching. Besides, if IFE occurs, the adsorption bands of the absorber have sufficient spectral overlap with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore. The results in Figure S4 revealed that the UV–vis absorption band of CLZ overlapped with the excitation and/or emission wavelength of B-CDs, demonstrating that IFE was one of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms. To further explore whether the static quenching mechanism occurred, Stern-Volmer equation was used, whose equation was shown in eq(1) below.
(1) F0/F = 1 + Ksv[c] = 1 + Kqτ0[c]
where τ0 was the average lifetime of B-CDs. Kq was the quenching rate constant. Ksv was the quenching constant. As shown in Fig. 5 B and Table S2 , Ksv decreased with the increase of the temperature. In temperature range of 20–40 ℃, the Kq was determined as 3.07 × 1013 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 to 2.92 × 1013 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 (The fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs was 2.19 ns), and the numerical values It is much larger than the maximum Kq of collision quenching (1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1). These phenomena indicate that static quenching may be another fluorescence quenching mechanism.

In addition, Zeta potential of B-CDs solution with and without CLZ were also measured. As described in Fig. 5 C, with the introduction of CLZ, the Zeta potential of B-CDs aqueous solution ranged from 1.229 to 13.7 mV. The reason for this phenomenon may be that CLZ combined with the carboxyl group or hydroxyl group on the surface of B-CDs to form a non-luminous coordination compound, which consumed the negative charge on the surface of B-CDs. In order to further investigate which functional group in the CLZ structure interacted with carboxyl group or hydroxyl group on B-CDs, and to analyze why interfering substances such as diazepam, cocaine and midazolam with similar structures did not have similar effects of fluorescence quenching effects of B-CDs, ESP profiles were calculated by DFT process. As shown in Figure S5 , the negatively charged region was shown in blue, while the positively charged region was shown in red. It can be found that the –COOH in B-CDs appeared blue (negatively charged) and the –NH at the 5th position on the surface of CLZ appeared red (positive charged). These two groups can interact with each other through weak electrostatic interaction and strong hydrogen bond interaction, resulting in forming non-fluorescent ground state complexes (static quenching) and quenching the fluorescence of B-CDs. However, compared with CLZ, although other drugs have similar N-atom structure, but because there was no –NH group in their structure. Although they had weak electrostatic interactions with B-CDs, they cannot combine with –COOH on the surface of B-CDs to form hydrogen bond. Therefore, it can be speculated the difference in the group of –NH at the 5th position of CLZ with other drugs may lead to the fluorescence quenching difference.

To explore the toxicity toward the cells, MTT measurement was conducted on HepG2 cells. As exhibited in Figure S6 , the cell viability of cells remained over 85% with the concentration of B-CDs reaching 400 μg/mL, demonstrating the low cell toxicity of the as-prepared B-CDs. Thus, the B-CDs may have good potential for subsequent application in biological systems.

In summary, B-CDs with QY of 38% were synthesized by using carbonized human hair as raw material through one-step dialysis method. The preparation process of B-CDs was simple, efficient, energy efficient and environmentally friendly. B-CDs showed outstanding acid and base stability, photostability, and fluorescence characteristics etc., thus, they can be used as the fluorescence sensor for the detection of CLZ. The quenching effect of CLZ showed good linear relationship in the concentration of 200 ng/mL–100 μg/mL with a low LOD of 67 ng/mL. Furthermore, in the analysis of actual tablets and blood sample, the content of CLZ in tablet and the concentration in blood sample which determined by the as-developed fluorescence method showed satisfactory recoveries. The results were highly consistent with the results of HPLC method, revealing the accuracy of the B-CDs based fluorescence method. In addition, the results of the cytotoxicity indicated that the B-CDs also showed great potential in the field of biological system.

Yongqing Tao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Runlan Wan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Junji Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Qiuyi Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Meng Tian: Data curation, Investigation. Luchun Wang: . Yulian Yang: Data curation, Investigation. Yuemeng Zou: Software, Investigation. Yuanning Luo: Software, Investigation. Famin Ke: Software, Investigation. Quan Zhou: Software, Investigation. Dandan Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan (Grant No. 23NSFSC5092), a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122803.
